--- SOURCE 1: Wide Bandgap Technology Enhances Performance of ... ---
URL: https://www.nrel.gov/transportation/wide-bandgap-technology

SUMMARY:
Wide Bandgap Technology Enhances Performance of Electric-Drive Vehicles | Transportation and Mobility Research | NREL  NREL researches wide bandgap (WBG) power electronics devices, which reduce component size, increase efficiency, and improve performance for hybrid and all-electric vehicles. Present-day silicon-based traction drive inverters for electric-drive vehicle (EDV) applications require two separate radiators and thermal management systems for the electric-drive components and the conventional internal combustion engine (ICE). NREL collaborated with Wolfspeed, Toyota, the National Center for Reliable Electric Power Transmission at the University of Arkansas, and DOE's Vehicle Technologies Office on this project, providing expertise for the thermal management, thermomechanical, and reliability aspects of the inverter development. Compared with silicon-based components, WBG materials/devices enable lighter, more compact, and more efficient power electronics for electrical power transfer applications—such as vehicle drive systems and solar inverters.

Wide Bandgap Technology Enhances Performance of Electric-Drive Vehicles
=======================================================================

NREL researches wide bandgap (WBG) power electronics devices, which reduce component size, increase efficiency, and improve performance for hybrid and all-electric vehicles.

![Image 2: A drawing of the DC-Link Capacitor with a circuit board on the left hand side and 3-D rectangular neon-colored boxes to the right.](https://www.nrel.gov/images/libraries/research-images/graphic-wide-bandgap-rendering.jpg?sfvrsn=8c44478b_1)

DC-link capacitor and busbar temperatures._Graphic by Gilbert Moreno, NREL_

Present-day silicon-based traction drive inverters for electric-drive vehicle (EDV) applications require two separate radiators and thermal management systems for the electric-drive components and the conventional internal combustion engine (ICE). This added weight, bulk, complexity, cost, and lack of efficiency is one hurdle to widespread consumer adoption of hybrid electric and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles in a competitive market dominated by ICE vehicles.

WBG-based components, incorporating[devices made from materials such as silicon carbide](https://www.nrel.gov/manufacturing/wide-bandgap-power-modules), can reduce power electronics component size and potentially reduce system or component-level cost, while improving performance and reliability, to give EDVs a competitive advantage in the marketplace. NREL researchers are characterizing the thermal performance and reliability of these emerging WBG-based packaging technologies that could potentially operate more efficiently and reliably under higher temperatures than current silicon-based components. NREL's ongoing research and development of thermal management strategies for WBG power electronics systems led to the world's first commercial high-temperature (140° C ambient), high-power density automotive traction drive inverter.

NREL collaborated with Wolfspeed, Toyota, the National Center for Reliable Electric Power Transmission at the University of Arkansas, and DOE's Vehicle Technologies Office on this project, providing expertise for the thermal management, thermomechanical, and reliability aspects of the inverter development. This resulting R&D 100 Award-winning technology is now being utilized by industry in components such as Wolfspeed's High-Temperature, Wide Bandgap Underhood Inverter for EDVs.

The WBG inverter developed by Wolfspeed in partnership with NREL has proven to be a smaller, lighter, higher power density, and more efficient system in comparison to the silicon-based inverter design used in EDVs currently on the road.

### Key Research Results

#### Achievement

Compared with silicon-based components, WBG materials/devices enable lighter, more compact, and more efficient power electronics for electrical power transfer applications—such as vehicle drive systems and solar inverters.

#### Key Result

The High-Temperature, Wide Bandgap Underhood Inverter developed by Wolfspeed in partnership with NREL outperforms silicon-based inverters and has been recognized with an R&D 100 Award.

#### Potential Impact

The commercialization of WBG technology can lead to increased electrification of multiple high-performance, low-cost vehicles, and increased electric vehicle adoption by consumers.

# Application of Wide Bandgap Devices in Electric Vehicle Converters

![bike-926037_1280](https://www.icdrex.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/bike-926037_1280-1024x683.jpg)

## Introduction

With the rapid development of the electric vehicle (EV) market, there is an increasing demand for efficient, lightweight, and high-power-density power electronic systems. Traditional silicon (Si)-based power devices have gradually become difficult to meet these requirements, while wide bandgap (WBG) semiconductor materials, such as silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN), have been widely used in electric vehicle converters due to their superior performance.

![car-6943508_1280](https://www.icdrex.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/car-6943508_1280-1024x640.jpg)

![car-6943508_1280](https://www.icdrex.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/car-6943508_1280-1024x640.jpg)

## 1. Characteristics of Wide Bandgap Semiconductors

Compared with traditional silicon-based semiconductors, wide bandgap materials have the following key advantages:

## 2. Application of Wide Bandgap Devices in Electric Vehicle Converters

In the power electronics system of electric vehicles, inverters, DC-DC converters, and on-board chargers (OBCs) are the main power conversion modules. Due to their excellent performance, SiC and GaN are gradually replacing traditional Si devices, improving system efficiency and reducing energy loss.

### 2.1 Traction Inverter

The traction inverter is responsible for converting the DC power of the high-voltage battery into AC power to drive the motor. Traditional Si IGBTs (insulated gate bipolar transistors) are still widely used in high-voltage, high-power density applications, but their switching speed is slow, resulting in high switching losses and large size.

Traction inverters using SiC MOSFETs can significantly reduce conduction losses and switching losses and support higher switching frequencies. This not only improves the energy efficiency of electric vehicles but also reduces the size of the inverter heat sink, thereby reducing the weight of the entire vehicle and increasing the driving range. For example, the Tesla Model 3 uses SiC MOSFET inverters, which improves its energy efficiency by more than 5% compared to traditional IGBT solutions.

### 2.2 DC-DC Converters

The DC-DC converters in electric vehicles are mainly used to step down high-voltage batteries (such as 400V or 800V) to 12V or 48V to power on-board electronic devices (such as lighting, entertainment systems, and electronic control units).

GaN devices perform well in DC-DC converters, especially in high-frequency, high-power-density application scenarios. Compared with Si and SiC, GaN has a higher switching speed, which allows DC-DC converters to use smaller inductors and capacitors, thereby reducing size and increasing power density. For example, a high-frequency DC-DC converter using GaN FETs can reduce the volume by more than 50% compared to traditional Si solutions while improving energy efficiency by 10% to 20%.

### 2.3 On-Board Charger (OBC)

The on-board charger (OBC) is an important part of the electric vehicle charging system, usually using a combination of AC-DC conversion and DC-DC conversion. Traditional Si solutions are limited by efficiency and heat dissipation in high-power OBC designs, while SiC and GaN solutions can provide higher energy efficiency and smaller size.

SiC MOSFET performs well in high-power OBC designs of 6.6kW and above, increasing conversion efficiency to more than 96% and reducing heat dissipation requirements. On the other hand, GaN also shows its advantages in OBC solutions below 3.3kW, making OBC smaller and lighter, suitable for compact electric vehicles.

## 

## 3. Future Development Trends

With the development of electric vehicle technology, wide bandgap semiconductor devices have broad application prospects, and the following development trends may appear in the future:

![buses-7094811_1280](https://www.icdrex.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/buses-7094811_1280-1024x682.jpg)

![buses-7094811_1280](https://www.icdrex.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/buses-7094811_1280-1024x682.jpg)

## 4. Conclusion

Wide bandgap semiconductor technology is accelerating the performance improvement of electric vehicles. SiC and GaN have demonstrated excellent performance advantages in key power electronic systems such as traction inverters, DC-DC converters, and on-board chargers. In the future, with the development of high-voltage architectures for electric vehicles and the reduction of WBG device costs, SiC and GaN are expected to become mainstream power devices, further promoting the electric vehicle industry to develop in a more efficient and lightweight direction.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Wide-bandgap (WBG) semiconductors, such as SiC and GaN, enhance electric vehicle performance by enabling efficient power conversion and reducing energy losses in key systems.

WBG-based traction inverters significantly improve energy efficiency, reduce size and weight, and support higher switching frequencies, leading to enhanced vehicle performance and extended driving range.
The increased efficiency of WBG components allows for simplified cooling systems, reducing thermal management complexity and associated costs while enabling smaller, lighter vehicle designs.
